text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Fiona  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Fiona,                                                                                                         

Thomas Reed, DOB: 22-11-1991, Hospital No: R528410 NHS No. 8742153690  
Flat 3, 45 Bloomsbury Way, London, WC1A 2SE  

Epilepsy – Focal Epilepsy Type

Medication: Previously on Levetiracetam up to 1,500 mg twice daily (stopped due to mood irritability); prior trial of Carbamazepine 400 mg twice daily (discontinued for hyponatraemia). Currently taking Lacosamide 150 mg twice daily and Zonisamide 200 mg nocte. Uses Buccal Midazolam 10 mg PRN for prolonged events (not required in the last 6 months). 

Lacosamide Blood Level:  
18th September 2025: 6.1 µg/mL (reference 1–10 µg/mL). Dose 150 mg twice daily.  

Seizures: He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically four episodes monthly. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable.

I reviewed Thomas Reed today in face-to-face clinic following reports of increased difficulties on workdays. He has a confirmed diagnosis of focal epilepsy arising from the left temporal region on prior EEG and semiology. Over the past three months he describes a deterioration in day-to-day functioning, with more frequent clustering of focal events Monday–Thursday, coinciding with periods of high workload and extended screen time. He notes brief post-event fatigue and word-finding pauses (Ã‚Â·), though he recovers without assistance. Sleep is fragmented during deadlines and missed meals are common. There have been no injuries, tongue bites, or incontinence. No recent intercurrent illness, alcohol excess, or missed doses reported; however, he admits to taking evening Zonisamide later than planned on late shifts (ï¿½). 

Examination today was non-focal neurologically; BP 128/76, pulse 68 regular. Weight stable at 74 kg. Mood mildly anxious by report; no suicidal ideation. Adverse effects: intermittent paraesthesiae in fingers thought possibly Zonisamide-related; no diplopia, ataxia, or notable dizziness. 

Work and safety: Events tend to cluster on workdays per patient report, with two episodes occurring at his desk in the last month, each lasting ~60–90 seconds and characterised by behavioural arrest, staring, and subsequent brief disorientation (â€). He has informed his line manager and is currently avoiding lone high-risk tasks; he does not drive and remains compliant with DVLA guidance. 

Investigations: Latest routine bloods (U&E, LFTs, bicarbonate) on 18/09/2025 normal; bicarbonate 23 mmol/L. Repeat EEG and MRI (3T epilepsy protocol) were previously performed in 2023 showing left mesial temporal sclerosis; no interval imaging since. 

Discussion today: Although caregivers perceive overall control as acceptable, both patient and clinician note a deterioration in functional impact with weekday clustering and greater post-ictal fatigue. Given the temporal association with workload and suboptimal sleep, we discussed structured sleep hygiene, scheduled meal breaks, and stress-modulation strategies. He will continue a seizure diary with specific annotation of work-related triggers and timing (Ã©). We also discussed the option of optimisation of current anti-seizure medication in due course and will obtain a Zonisamide level to guide any future adjustments. 

Plan:  
- Continue Lacosamide 150 mg twice daily and Zonisamide 200 mg nocte for now.  
- Check Zonisamide trough level and repeat U&E/bicarbonate in 6 weeks.  
- Arrange ambulatory EEG to capture weekday clustering.  
- Occupational health referral to consider temporary workload adjustments and regular protected breaks.  
- Provide written seizure first-aid guidance to employer; patient to avoid lone work at heights or near open water/unguarded machinery (ï¿½).  
- Telephone review in 8 weeks; earlier if seizure frequency increases or any generalised events occur.  

I will update you following results. Please let me know if any concerns are raised by his workplace or caregivers in the interim.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically four episodes monthly.' This provides a clear numeric frequency. There are mentions of clustering on workdays and two specific episodes at his desk in the last month, but these do not supersede the explicit typical monthly frequency. No generalized tonic-clonic seizures since last year does not indicate seizure freedom. Therefore, the highest/clearest stated frequency is four per month.
seizure_frequency_number: He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically four episodes monthly.
reference: He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically four episodes monthly. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable
